title: Polymerization of molten salt monomers having a phenylimidazolium group
abstract:
Polymerization of Molten Salt Monomers
having a Phenylimidazolium Group
Michiko Hirao,Kaori Ito-Akita and Hiroyuki Ohno*
Department of Biotechnology, Tokyo University of Agriculture and Technology, Koganei, Tokyo 184-8588, Japan
ABSTRACT

Several kinds of imidazolium salts having a phenyl group
were prepared to investigate the property of room
temperature molten salts. N-Alkylimidazoles were neu-
tralized to obtain mono-alkylimidazolium salts (RImX)
as model compounds of 1,3-dialkylimidazolium salts
(R,R'ImX). Some of RImX salts formed room tempera-
ture molten salts. According to the data of RImX, alkyl
group was selected for the design of R,R'ImX. R,R'ImX
obtained through this procedure showed relatively high
ionic conductivity about 10ÿ4 S cmÿ1 at 30°C. The
polymers having these molten salt structures in the side
chains were also prepared. The ionic conductivity of the
obtained polymers in the bulk decreased to 10ÿ6 S cmÿ1
at 30°C. When lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)i-
mide (LiTFSI) was added equimolarly to polymers, the
glass transition temperature (Tg) decreased and their
mechanical properties were improved from glassy solids
to adhesive film. The ionic conductivity was improved up
to 10ÿ5 S cmÿ1. The introduction of a phenyl group into
imidazolium salts was confirmed to be effective to
improve the film property of molten salt polymers.
Copyright 2000 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
KEYWORDS: imidazolium salt; neutralization; room
temperature molten salt; ionic conductivity; molten salt
polymer
INTRODUCTION

Solid polymer electrolytes have been prepared
mostly by polyether-based materials like poly(-
ethylene oxide) [1±3]. Ionic conductivity generally
depends on the number of dissociated ions and
their mobility in the polymer. In order to improve
ionic conductivity, it is required to increase both or
either of them. The ionic conductivity of polyether-
based polymer electrolytes, however, decreased by
adding organic or inorganic lithium salts because
of strong ion-dipole interaction between ether
oxygens in the polymer backbone and lithium ions
[4]. To overcome such drawbacks, many groups
have tried to design a new type of polymer
electrolyte [1] and salts [5±8].
Room temperature molten salts or ionic liquids
such as imidazolium, pyridinium and ammonium
salts have the desirable properties in terms of their
high mobility and ion concentration. Attention has
been paid especially to imidazolium salts because
they exhibit the most remarkable properties such as
high ionic conductivity, low viscosity, and electro-
chemical stability among other room temperature
molten salts [9]. Recently, non-aluminum chloride
type imidazolium salts have been investigated as
non-aqueous solutions for lithium ion batteries,
capacitors and so on [10±18]. Since it was expected
that the domains based on imidazolium salts kept
the matrix mobility high and hopefully increased
the number of carrier ions, we tried to introduce the
structure of imidazolium type room temperature
molten salts into the polymers [19±21]. According
to our previous work [19±21], imidazolium salts are
classified-into two groups. One is conventional
imidazolium
salt;
1,3-dialkylimidazolium
salt

 (R,R'ImX). The other is a new type of imidazolium
salt; mono-alkylimidazolium salt (RImX) prepared
from the neutralization of various kinds of N-
monoalkylimidazoles and corresponding acids. As
many types of N-monoalkylimidazoles are avail-
able commercially, therefore the design and pre-
paration of RImX is very easy. Moreover, RImX
salts were obtained in high yield and purity, and
instantly obtained because there were no tedious
steps and by-products in their synthesis and
purification processes. In our previous study, some
of RImX with BF4
ÿ andCF3SO3
ÿ was confirmed to
form molten salts at ambient temperature [22]. In
addition, the ionic conductivity of these RImX was
high or equivalent to that for R,R'ImX. For these
reasons, we proposed that RImX were useful as a
new type of room temperature molten salts and as
the model compounds for R,R'ImX.
We have already reported the imidazolium type
molten salt polymers, poly(mono-alkylimidazo-
lium salt) [P(RImX)] [21] and poly(1,3-dialkylimi-
dazolium salt) [P(R,R'ImX)] [20]. These molten salt
polymers were expected to have flexible domain
derived from imidazolium salts, and these materi-
als
showed
excellent
ionic
conductivity
after
equimolar mixing with lithium bis(trifluorometha-
nesulfonyl)imide
(LiTFSI).
Ionic
conductivity
reached about 1.3  10ÿ5 and 7.5  10ÿ6 S cmÿ1 at
30°C, respectively. It is effective for highly ion
conductive polymers to incorporate imidazolium
structure into the polymer matrix. However, the
molten salt polymers previously prepared were
rubber-like or brittle material, the film formation
was quite difficult. RImX with phenylimidazolium
cations formed room temperature molten salts
having relatively high ionic conductivity in spite
of a bulky molecular structure [22]. This result led
us to introduce a polystyrene structure into the

Department of Biotechnology, Tokyo University of Agriculture and Technology, Koganei, Tokyo 184-8588, Japan

Several kinds of imidazolium salts having a phenyl group

improve the film property of molten salt polymers.

reached about 1.3  10ÿ5 and 7.5  10ÿ6 S cmÿ1 at
30°C, respectively. It is effective for highly ion
conductive polymers to incorporate imidazolium
structure into the polymer matrix. However, the

cations formed room temperature molten salts

Polymerization of Molten Salt Monomers / 535
imidazolium salts, expecting an improvement of
mechanical strength of the molten salt polymers.
In this study, we showed a new type of molten
salt polymer containing a phenyl group, of which
monomer structure was optimized through the
experiments with RImX as model compounds.
Then, several R,R'ImX type monomers were poly-
merized. The properties of these prepared poly-

The two series of imidazolium salts having phenyl
group were prepared; series a contains either
benzene sulfonate anion (1a) or p-toluenesulfonate
anion and series b contains 1-benzylimidazolium
1-Ethylimidazole (EtIm) and 1-benzyl-2-methy-
limidazole (1-Bz-2-MeIm) were reacted equimo-
larly with benzensulfonic acid or HBF4 to obtain 1a
and 1b, respectively. In order to obtain R,R'ImX
having similar structure to 1a and 1b, 2a and 2b
were prepared by the ethylation of EtIm and 1-Bz-
2-MeIm using ethyl p-toluenesulfonate (EtTos) and
bromoethane in dimethylformamide (DMF) for 1
day at room temperature. Both 3a and 3b were the
starting monomers for molten salt polymer 4a and
4b. Thus 3a and 3b were prepared from 1-
vinylimidazole and 1-ethylimidazole by the reac-
tion with EtTos or chloromethylstyrene, respec-
tively. Through the anion exchange reaction with
NH4BF4 or lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyli-
mide) (LiTFSI) 2b and 3b were obtained. These 3a
and 3b were polymerized with 2,2'-azobis(isobu-
tyronitrile) (AIBN) (5 mol% to the vinyl unit) as a
acetone at 70°C for 3 h. After drying, 4a and 4b
were obtained as transparent glassy solids.
The ionic conductivity measurement was carried
out with Schlumberger Solartron 1260 impedance/
gain-phase analyzer with the frequency range
10 Hz±10 MHz. The impedance data were analyzed
by the complex impedance method. The tempera-
Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) mea-
surement was carried out with a DSC-120 (SEIKO
Instrument Inc.) under nitrogen atmosphere. All
samples were cooled down by liquid nitrogen from
room temperature to ÿ150°C, and DSC was carried
out at the heating rate of 10°Cminÿ1 to 200°C.
WWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWW
Table 1 shows thermal and electrical properties of
phenylimidazolium type molten salts. 2a and 2b
also formed room temperature molten salts as well
as 1a and 1b and had relatively high ionic
conductivity. The ionic conductivity of R,R'ImX
2a and 2b was higher than that of RImX; 1a and 1b.
The difference of the ionic conductivity was
suggested to be caused by the difference in the
strength of electrostatic ion±ion interactions. The
strength of the interaction between imidazolium
ring protons and their anions was previously
analyzed using 1H-NMR measurement [13]. From
the results, the hydrogen bonding between hydro-
gen atom at the 2-position of the imidazolium ring
and anion was stronger than that between protons
derived from substituent alkyl chain. Stronger
interaction between 1a or 1b and their anions
caused lower mobility of the matrix and lower ionic
conductivity than 2a and 2b. The introduction of a
room temperature molten salt structure like 2a and
2b into the polymer was, therefore, expected to be
effective for high ionic conductivity.
On the basis of the above results, monomeric
R,R'ImX 3a and 3b having a vinyl group were
prepared. Figure 1 shows the temperature depen-
dence of the ionic conductivity of 3a and 3b. The
ionic conductivity of 3a decreased about three
orders compared to that of model compound 2a
because 3a had a melting temperature (Tm) at
82.9°C. Crystallization of matrix suppressed the
carrier ions' migration considerably. However, 3b
temperature range, therefore high ionic conductiv-
ity equal to 2b was observed. It was still not clear
why the ionic conductivity of 3a was lower than
that of 2a by means of the difference between ethyl
TABLE 1. Properties of Phenyl Imidazolium Salts
FIGURE 1. Temperature dependence of ionic conductivity
of monomeric imidazolium salts (3a and 3b) and molten
salt polymers (4a and 4b). *: 3a, ◊: 3b, *: 4a/100
mol% of LiTFSI, ^: 4b/100 mol% of LiTFSI.

Polym. Adv. Technol. 11, 534±538 (2000)
The polymerization of monomer 3a increases
the viscosity, it should inhibit the crystallization.
Therefore, we tried to polymerize both 3a and 3b to
obtain molten salts polymers. Figure 1 also shows
temperature dependence of ionic conductivity of 4a
and 4b containing same molar ratio of lithium salt
to imidazolium cation (100 mol%), respectively.
After the polymerization, the mobility and the
number of carrier ions of 4a and 4b decreased
because 4a and 4b were covalently bound to form
polycations, which were glassy solids. Their ionic
conductivity decreased to about 10ÿ6 S cmÿ1 or less
in the bulk at 30°C. After mixing the same molar
ratio of LiTFSI, their ionic conductivity was greatly
improved. When 100 mol% of LiTFSI was added to
4a, the matrix became viscous liquid. However, 4b
formed an adhesive film by adding 100 mol% of
LiTFSI. In the case of 4b, the distance between
imidazolium cation and the backbone of the
polymer was longer than that of 4a because
imidazolium cation was bound to a phenyl group
derived from poly(styrene). Poly(styrene) structure
was effective to improve the mechanical property
although 4a became a viscous liquid after the
addition of LiTFSI. In addition, the ionic conduc-
tivity of 4b/100 mol% of LiTFSI was relatively high
in spite of their rigidity. It was suggested that
appropriate distance between imidazolium cations
and polymer-chain caused high mobility of molten
salt domains and fast migration of carrier ions.
The effects of salt concentration on the ionic
conductivity of 4a and 4b were analyzed. Figure 2
shows ionic conductivity of 4a with p-toluenesul-
fonate anion (Tosÿ) as a function of lithium salt
concentration. The ionic conductivity was im-
proved by the addition of lithium salts. In the case
of 4a, higher ionic conductivity and lower glass
transition temperature (Tg) were observed when
LiTFSI was added. It was concerned that LiTFSI
acted as a plasticizer [23] or the anion exchange
reaction from Tosÿ into TFSIÿ was carried out in
the side chain by mixing with LiTFSI. In the
previous reports, the melting points of R,R'ImX
were revealed to be affected by anion species. For
example, 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium salts (Et-
MeImX) having TFSIÿ showed lower Tm and
kinetic viscosity () than R,R'ImX having BFÿ
13]. The values of Tm and  for EtMeImTFSI were
ÿ3°C and 34 cP although EtMeImBF4 showed 15°C
and 42 cP, respectively. It was suggested that TFSIÿ
was effective in decreasing Tm or glass formation
(no Tm). Lower Tm caused higher mobility of
imidazolium salts, which enabled fast carrier ion
migration in the polymer matrix. When LiTFSI was
added to 4a, the mechanical property of 4a changed
from glassy solid to viscous liquid, therefore,
carrier ions were able to migrate fast in the polymer
matrix compared with the case of the addition of
LiBF4 to 4a. When the LiTFSI concentration was 100
mol% or more, the ionic conductivity was kept
nearly constant. It was suggested that the mobility
of the polymer matrix was enough to keep the
migration of carrier ions high because their Tg
The dependence of lithium salt concentration
for ionic conductivity of 4b having poly(benzyli-
midazolium) is also shown in Fig. 2. Ionic con-
ductivity of 4b was increased up to 100 mol% of
LiTFSI. A slight decrease of ionic conductivity was
observed when more than 100 mol% of the salt was
added. The increase of ionic conductivity less than
100 mol% of LiTFSI was comprehensible as the
increase of the number of carrier ions. Up to this
concentration, the salt addition was revealed to be
effective. However, Tg of 4b/100 mol% of LiTFSI
was ÿ50°C while that of 4b/300 mol% of LiTFSI
was elevated up to ÿ40°C. The addition of an
excess salt to 4b caused the decrease of the mobility
showing poor ionic conductivity.

RImX salts having a phenyl group formed room
temperature molten salts. Then, R,R'ImX salts
containing similar structure to RImX were pre-
pared. These R,R'ImX showed relatively high ionic
conductivity about 4.5  10ÿ4 S cmÿ1 (containing p-
toluenesulfonate anion) and 1.6  10ÿ4 S cmÿ1 (1-
showed the molten salt polymer with the domains
derived from R,R'ImX. Their ionic conductivity
was improved by mixing of equimolar of LiTFSI up
to 3.0  10ÿ4 and 1.0  10ÿ5 S cmÿ1. In the case of a
molten salt polymer having a poly(styrene) struc-
ture, it formed an adhesive film. It was suggested
that the introduction of a poly(styrene) structure
into the polymer matrix was effective for molten
salt polymer with high film forming ability and
